sections.heading: Life, Conversion, and Method
sections.html:
From Atheism to Reluctant Belief

Clive Staples Lewis (1898–1963) began his intellectual life as a convinced atheist, shaped by the materialism of his tutor W.T. Kirkpatrick and the apparent meaninglessness of a world that had taken his mother when he was nine years old. His path toward belief was neither sudden nor sentimental but ran through the history of philosophy itself. While studying at Oxford he encountered British Idealism, which forced him to take seriously the category of the Absolute — a move that cracked open the purely materialist frame. By 1929 he had arrived at Theism, kneeling in his room at Magdalen College and acknowledging God as God, describing himself afterward as perhaps the most reluctant convert in all England. Two years later, in September 1931, a late-night conversation with J.R.R. Tolkien and Hugo Dyson on the topic of myth and meaning carried him the final distance to Christianity. Tolkien's argument was that the Gospel story functioned as a myth that had actually happened — the Incarnation as the true fulfillment of every human longing encoded in ancient story. Lewis received this as illumination rather than argument and shortly afterward accepted the full Christian claim.

This conversion biography matters apologetically because Lewis never forgot what it felt like to be outside the faith and to consider it intellectually beneath contempt. His entire apologetic project was written for that person — the intelligent, serious unbeliever who needed philosophy, not sentiment.

The Apologetic Method

Lewis employed several interlocking arguments rather than a single system. The argument from reason held that naturalism — the view that the physical universe is all there is — defeats itself: if our minds are nothing but the products of non-rational natural processes, there is no ground for trusting them to deliver truth, including the truth of naturalism. Reason, as such, cannot be a mere product of nature; it points beyond nature to a rational ground for reality itself.

Alongside this ran the argument from desire, which Lewis called by its German name Sehnsucht — an inconsolable longing that no earthly object ever finally satisfies. Beauty, romantic love, music, friendship, nature — all awaken it and all disappoint it upon arrival. Lewis argued that this structural feature of human desire is best explained by the hypothesis that we were made for a joy that lies beyond the created order. The pattern is familiar in Scripture: Ps 42:1 names the thirst; Eccl 3:11 observes that God has placed eternity in the human heart.

The moral argument built on the observation that human beings across every culture appeal to a standard of right and wrong they themselves acknowledge — a Law they did not invent and frequently disobey. The existence of such a universal moral law implies a Moral Lawgiver who stands behind it, a conclusion Paul draws in Rom 2:14–15.

The Trilemma

Lewis's most famous single argument concerns the identity of Jesus. He observed that Jesus made claims to divine identity that rule out the comfortable modern option of regarding him merely as a great moral teacher. When Jesus declared his pre-existence before Abraham (John 8:58), identified himself with the Father (John 10:30), and accepted Peter's confession that he was the Christ, the Son of the living God (Matt 16:13–16), he was not leaving neutral ground available. A person who makes such claims is either deliberately deceiving — a liar of the worst order — or genuinely deluded about his own identity — a lunatic — or the claims are true, and he is Lord. Lewis pressed the point that the actual character of Jesus as recorded in the Gospels simply does not fit the profile of a liar or a madman. The third option stands. This argument does not close the door to scholarly debate about the Gospel texts, but it closes the door to intellectual respectability for the “good teacher only” response.

sections.heading: The Major Works
sections.html:
Mere Christianity (1952)

Originating as a series of BBC radio broadcasts during the Second World War, Mere Christianity is Lewis's most systematic apologetic and theological statement. He opened with the moral argument — the universal human experience of a Law above us — and built from there to God, to the dilemma of human sin, and to the Person and work of Jesus Christ. The book is structured in three movements: the existence of right and wrong as evidence for God; the Christian doctrine of God including the Trinity; and the shape of Christian character. Lewis employed a spatial metaphor for the church: mere Christianity is the hallway into which one first enters; the various denominations are the rooms off the hall. He urged readers to enter a room — to commit to a living tradition of worship and sacrament — rather than to live permanently in the corridor.

His treatment of the Atonement deliberately resisted reducing it to a single theory, but he gave central place to the image of enemy-occupied territory: the world belongs to God but has been seized by a hostile power, and the Incarnation is the entry of the rightful King in disguise to win it back. His chapter on the New Man remains one of the most theologically rich discussions of sanctification and transformation in popular Christian writing.

The Problem of Pain (1940)

In this earlier work Lewis addressed the intellectual problem of suffering directly: how can a good and omnipotent God permit pain? His central moves were to argue that human goodness and divine goodness are not the same thing — God's love is not indulgence but the love of an artist for the perfection of his work — and that free will provides a necessary context for genuine moral existence. A world with no possibility of harm would be a world with no real persons, no real love, and no real virtue.

Lewis further argued that suffering functions as what he called the divine megaphone: the comfortable soul does not seek God; pain strips away the illusions of self-sufficiency and forces the question of where our true good lies. Paul's language in Rom 5:3–5 — that tribulation produces perseverance, proven character, and hope — and the author of Hebrews' reflection that God disciplines those he loves for their profit that they may share in his holiness (Heb 12:10–11) stand behind Lewis's argument. He also devoted a chapter to animal pain, acknowledging it as the hardest part of the problem and offering a tentative rather than definitive response.

The Screwtape Letters (1942) and Miracles (1947)

The Screwtape Letters approached apologetics from below: by imagining the correspondence of a senior devil coaching a junior one in the art of human corruption, Lewis exposed the mechanics of spiritual failure. The demonic strategy is not primarily dramatic temptation but patient distraction — keeping the human creature from attending to what is real, filling life with noise and appetite so that the still small voice never breaks through. The book is a sustained meditation on how Christian faith erodes in practice rather than collapses in argument.

Miracles returned to the philosophical front to address naturalism directly and at greater length than in Mere Christianity. Lewis argued that the objection to miracles assumes naturalism, but naturalism cannot justify itself on its own terms (the argument from reason). Given a personal God who created nature, miracles are not violations of the natural order but the action of the Author entering the story — an occurrence that, far from breaking the narrative, is the decisive plot event around which everything else is organized. The Incarnation and Resurrection are therefore the most reasonable of all claims about the universe if there is a God, not the least.

sections.heading: Theology and Legacy
sections.html:
Christology, Salvation, and the Inklings

Lewis's theology was deliberately ecumenical at the level of foundational doctrine. He was an Anglican who received communion regularly — daily in later life — and who held a high view of the sacraments while refusing to make sacramental theology the ground of his apologetics. His Christology was entirely orthodox: the eternal Son who became flesh, died for sin, rose bodily, and now reigns. His understanding of salvation made room for both the objective transaction of the cross and the subjective transformation of the believer into the likeness of Christ. He was uncomfortable with any account of the faith that reduced salvation to a moment of decision without the subsequent life of discipleship and growing conformity to Christ.

The Inklings — the informal Oxford literary group that met weekly during the 1930s and 1940s — provided the intellectual and spiritual community within which much of Lewis's work was developed and tested. Among its members, J.R.R. Tolkien and Charles Williams were the most significant theological conversation partners. Dorothy Sayers, though not an Inkling, was a close correspondent and shared Lewis's conviction that orthodox Christian doctrine was intellectually more exciting, not less, than its liberal revisions.

Influence and Legacy

Lewis became, in the decades following his death, the most widely read Christian apologist of the twentieth century. His influence cuts across denominations precisely because his apologetics operated at the level of mere Christianity — the shared deposit of Trinitarian and incarnational faith — rather than at the level of confessional particulars. Philosophers such as Alvin Plantinga have acknowledged the force of the argument from reason; apologists such as Peter Kreeft have built entire careers in his tradition. The Chronicles of Narnia carried his theological imagination to generations of readers who would never encounter a formal argument, embedding the shape of the Gospel story — sacrifice, resurrection, the long defeat and final victory — in the narrative imagination of millions.

The scriptural spine of Lewis's entire project can be traced in three texts. The command to love God with all one's mind (Matt 22:37) underwrites his conviction that intellectual rigour is itself an act of worship. The testimony that God has made his invisible attributes plain in what he has made (Rom 1:19–20) grounds his apologetic use of nature and desire as pointers to God. And the exclusive claim of Jesus — that he is the way, the truth, and the life (John 14:6) — is the destination toward which all of Lewis's arguments are aimed.
